resume:
DIRECTOR OF CLINICAL OPERATIONS | PATIENT SAFETY | VALUE-BASED CARE | LEAN HEALTHCARE

Results-driven, patient-centric clinical operations executive leveraging cross-functional leadership to drive operational excellence, throughput optimization, and best-in-class quality outcomes across the care continuum.

CORE COMPETENCIES
Lean Six Sigma | Patient Throughput | Length-of-Stay Reduction | HCAHPS | CMS Star Ratings | Value-Based Care | Population Health | EHR Optimization (Epic) | Capacity Management | Care Coordination | Joint Commission Readiness | Change Management | Stakeholder Alignment | RCA / FMEA | Magnet Designation | Revenue Cycle | Throughput KPIs

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE
Director, Clinical Operations — Lakeshore Regional Medical Center (420-bed acute care) — 2020-Present
- Spearheaded enterprise-wide patient-flow transformation across the care continuum, driving best-in-class throughput and operational excellence
- Championed a culture of safety leveraging high-reliability principles and data-driven decisioning
- Orchestrated cross-functional alignment between nursing, case management, and physician stakeholders
- Operationalized capacity-command-center methodologies to optimize bed utilization end-to-end

Associate Director, Care Coordination — St. Brendan Health System — 2016-2020
- Drove length-of-stay initiatives and discharge-planning workflows leveraging Lean methodologies
- Partnered seamlessly with revenue-cycle and quality stakeholders to deliver value-based outcomes

Manager, Nursing Operations — St. Brendan Health System — 2012-2016

EDUCATION & CREDENTIALS
MSN, Nursing Administration | BSN | RN (active) | Lean Six Sigma Green Belt | CPHQ
jd: Northcrest Health is recruiting a Director of Clinical Operations to lead patient throughput and capacity management for a 500-bed academic medical center. Reporting to the COO, this leader owns the capacity command center, ED-to-inpatient flow, discharge optimization, and the relationship between nursing, hospitalists, and case management. The mandate is concrete: median ED boarding time currently exceeds 6 hours against a target of 2; observed-to-expected length of stay sits at 1.18 with a target of 1.0; and avoidable discharge delays cost an estimated $4M annually. You will be expected to stand up daily flow huddles, deploy a measurable capacity-management model, and demonstrate prior, personally-led results in length-of-stay reduction. We will probe for specifics: what you changed, what moved, and how you isolated your intervention's effect from secular trends. Joint Commission and Epic experience expected.

history.q: Your resume is heavy on themes like 'operational excellence' and 'best-in-class throughput.' Pick the single flow initiative you're proudest of and ground it for me.
What was the baseline metric before you started, and where did it land?
Walk me through what you and your team actually changed operationally to get there.

candidate_last_answer: So when I came into Lakeshore, our discharge process was a mess — patients medically ready to leave were sitting in beds until mid-afternoon because the orders, transport, and pharmacy reconciliation weren't sequenced. The goal I set with the COO was to pull our discharge-order-to-departure curve earlier in the day so we'd free beds for the ED before the afternoon surge. What we changed: I stood up a 9 a.m. multidisciplinary flow huddle with nursing, case management, hospitalists, and pharmacy, and we instituted a 'discharge by noon' target with the criteria-led discharge orders written the evening before. We also built a real-time bed-tracking board in Epic so charge nurses could see the queue. I personally chaired the huddle for the first four months until it became routine, and I retrained the charge nurses on the new escalation pathway. It genuinely changed the culture — people started owning the discharge instead of waiting on each other.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: pull our discharge-order-to-departure curve earlier in the day

expected-yield: the concrete before/after numbers the answer omits — baseline vs. post-intervention median discharge time and observed-to-expected LOS, the percentage of discharges hitting the by-noon target, and over what period the gain held
